cases.title: Not Just Drunk
cases.presentation: A man found outside a bar smells of alcohol and is slurring his words. Bystanders say he 'just had too much.' On exam you note a boggy scalp hematoma, and his level of consciousness is worsening over a few minutes.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate interpretation and action?
options: He is simply intoxicated—let him sleep it off
Suspect a serious head injury (the bradycardia, hypertension, scalp hematoma, and falling GCS) despite alcohol—manage as TBI and transport rapidly
cases.explanation: Alcohol odor does not explain a boggy scalp hematoma, bradycardia, hypertension, and a declining GCS—these suggest an expanding intracranial injury (Cushing's reflex). Never attribute deterioration to intoxication; check glucose, support the airway/oxygenation, and transport as a head injury.
cases.clinicalPearl: The smell of alcohol is not a diagnosis—an intoxicated patient who deteriorates has a head injury until proven otherwise.

cases.title: Excited Delirium
cases.presentation: Police request EMS for a man who is violently agitated, paranoid, sweating profusely, and stripping off his clothes after suspected stimulant use. He shows remarkable strength and his skin is hot to the touch.
cases.question: What is the correct approach?
options: Restrain him prone and leave him face-down until he tires
Recognize excited delirium as a medical emergency—coordinate rapid control, sedate per protocol, cool him, oxygenate, and monitor continuously
Ignore it; he will calm down
cases.explanation: Extreme agitation, paranoia, hyperthermia, and apparent superhuman strength after stimulant use indicate excited delirium, which can progress to sudden cardiovascular collapse. Treat it as a medical emergency: rapid, coordinated control, sedation per protocol, active cooling, oxygenation, and continuous monitoring—never prolonged prone restraint.
cases.clinicalPearl: Excited delirium kills—treat the hyperthermia and agitation aggressively, and never restrain the patient prone.

cases.title: Restraint Done Safely
cases.presentation: An impaired, combative trauma patient must be restrained for the safety of himself and the crew so you can manage a bleeding head wound.
cases.question: Which restraint practice is correct?
options: Place him prone in a hog-tie position
Restrain him supine or on his side, never compromising breathing, and continuously reassess his airway and circulation
Use the tightest possible restraints and leave him
cases.explanation: Restraint is appropriate only for safety and must be applied humanely without compromising breathing—never prone or hog-tied (positional asphyxia risk). Restrain supine or lateral, monitor continuously, and reassess airway, breathing, and circulation throughout.
cases.clinicalPearl: Safe restraint is never prone—position to protect breathing and keep monitoring the patient continuously.
